title: Balanced Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced. A height-balanced binary tree is a binary tree in which the depth of the two subtrees of every node never differs by more than one.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 5000]
thinking_questions: Definition: height-balanced = every node has left and right subtree heights differing by ≤1. Does this need to hold for every node or just the root?
Naive: compute height of every node separately — that's O(n) per node = O(n²). Can you compute balance and height simultaneously?
What should your DFS return to communicate both height and imbalance detection?

hints.L1: A tree is balanced only if EVERY node's subtrees differ by ≤1 in height. Check this in a single DFS by returning height or -1 for imbalanced.
hints.L2: DFS returns -1 if subtree is imbalanced, otherwise returns height. If either child returns -1, or |left-right|>1, return -1. Otherwise return 1+max(left,right).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: return -1 as sentinel for imbalanced; otherwise return height
def dfs(node):
    if not node: return 0
    left = dfs(node.left)
    right = dfs(node.right)
    if left == -1 or right == -1: return -1  # subtree imbalanced
    if abs(left - right) > 1: return -1      # this node imbalanced
    return 1 + max(left, right)
return dfs(root) != -1
# Time: O(n)  Space: O(h)

follow_up_questions: Why return -1 as a sentinel rather than a boolean?
What's the complexity of the naive approach (separate height call per node)?
Could you solve this top-down instead of bottom-up? What's the difference in complexity?
solution_notes: Bottom-up DFS: return -1 for imbalance, height otherwise. O(n). -1 sentinel avoids separate imbalanced flag.
